context.use_case: Care coordination platform for multi-site hospital network. Manages patient handoffs, care team communication, and readmission tracking across 8 hospitals.
context.tech_stack: Epic EHR, Microsoft Teams, Azure, Power BI, custom Node.js integrations
context.notes: Original champion (Dr. Sarah Kim, CMO) left the organization in January. Current contact is IT Director (James Tran) who wasn’t involved in original purchase. Usage dropped 40% since Q3. Renewal value: $380K ARR. Renewal date: June 1, 2026.

questions.text: Outside of care coordination, are there other departments or use cases where you’ve seen appetite for a tool like this?
questions.answer: ED triage team expressed interest last year but it never went anywhere. James said he’d be open to a conversation about it but needs to understand the current product better first. No active expansion discussions.
follow_ups: Was the ED interest driven by a specific pain point or just general curiosity?
Is there a budget owner for ED technology separate from IT?

questions.text: Have any of your 8 hospitals gone significantly deeper on adoption than the others?
questions.answer: Yes — Evergreen Main and Eastside are the power users. The other 6 are minimal. The difference seems to be that those two had engaged nurse champions during rollout who kept training up. The other sites didn’t have that.
follow_ups: What did those nurse champions do differently that drove adoption?
Is there any cross-site communication between power users and lagging sites?

questions.text: Is there interest in expanding to any outpatient or affiliated clinics beyond the hospital network?
questions.answer: Not a current priority. They’re focused on stabilizing within the 8 hospitals first. James was direct — expansion conversations feel premature given where adoption is today.
follow_ups: Is there a longer-term roadmap where outpatient coordination is a goal?
Who would own that decision — IT or clinical leadership?

questions.text: What does your growth plan look like over the next 12–18 months — any new facilities or service lines?
questions.answer: Potential acquisition of a regional clinic network in Q4 2026 but nothing confirmed yet. James mentioned it offhand — if it happens, they’d need to evaluate how the platform extends to the new sites.
follow_ups: Who’s driving the acquisition decision — is this C-suite led?
Would the clinic network come with its own tech stack that would need to be integrated?

questions.text: Help me understand the 40% drop in usage we’re seeing — was that a gradual decline or did something specific trigger it?
questions.answer: It started after Dr. Kim left in January. She ran weekly training sessions and was the internal champion who kept adoption visible. No one stepped in. James acknowledged this was an internal failure, not a product failure.
follow_ups: Did the usage drop happen immediately or over a few months?
Is leadership aware that the departure caused this, or is there any blame attributed to the product?

questions.text: How are clinical staff feeling about the platform today — are we hearing complaints, or is it more just unused?
questions.answer: Mixed. Nurses who use it regularly love it. The drop-off is mostly care coordinators who never fully adopted — there’s no executive pressure to use it so it’s fallen off the radar. Not negative sentiment, just apathy.
follow_ups: Have there been any formal feedback surveys or NPS-style input from clinical staff?
Is there a clinical informatics team that tracks tool adoption?

questions.text: What would need to happen for this to feel like a success story for Evergreen 12 months from now?
questions.answer: James said he needs to show measurable outcomes to his CFO. Specifically wants readmission rate data tied to platform usage, but he doesn’t know how to pull that from our system. He’s also mentioned wanting broader adoption across all 8 hospitals.
follow_ups: Is there a specific readmission rate target they’re working toward?
Would a before/after comparison from the two high-adoption sites be compelling?

questions.text: Is there any internal sentiment that the platform hasn’t delivered on what was promised during the sale?
questions.answer: Some. The original business case was built around Dr. Kim’s priorities. James has seen the contract but wasn’t part of the original pitch, so he doesn’t feel ownership over those goals. He’s essentially evaluating us fresh without the context of what was promised.
follow_ups: Does James have access to the original success metrics from the contract?
Would it help to do a formal business review to re-anchor expectations?

questions.text: Walk me through what a typical week looks like for a care coordinator using the platform.
questions.answer: At the 2 high-adoption sites, coordinators check the platform every morning for handoff alerts, update care notes 2–3x per day. At low-adoption sites, they log in maybe once a week if that — primarily to check on flagged patients.
follow_ups: Is there a difference in patient outcomes that’s visible between the high- and low-adoption sites?
What does the login/activity data show for the 6 lagging hospitals?

questions.text: What’s the most common reason you hear for not using the platform regularly?
questions.answer: Two main reasons: 1) Staff default to Teams for quick communication and don’t see the value in switching context. 2) The mobile app has been glitchy on older hospital iPads and IT hasn’t pushed the latest update yet.
follow_ups: Is there a way to surface the platform’s value within Teams itself — e.g. a bot or integration?
What’s the timeline on pushing the iPad update — is that within IT’s control?

questions.text: Are there features you purchased that your team isn’t using yet?
questions.answer: The analytics dashboard — they’ve never really used it. James didn’t know it existed until I mentioned it today. Also the Epic bi-directional sync has only been turned on for one hospital. The other 7 are still one-way.
follow_ups: What would it take to enable Epic bi-directional sync at the other 7 hospitals?
Would a guided walkthrough of the analytics dashboard be valuable — perhaps with James and Maria?

questions.text: What does onboarding look like for new hires — are they getting trained on the platform?
questions.answer: No formal process. New hires shadow existing staff, and if their team doesn’t use the platform, they don’t learn it. IT doesn’t own onboarding for clinical tools — it falls to department managers who have no playbook.
follow_ups: Is there a clinical education team or L&D function we could partner with to build onboarding materials?
Would Evergreen be open to a joint onboarding playbook we develop together?

questions.text: When does the renewal come up, and who has authority to sign?
questions.answer: Renewal is June 1, 2026. James has authority up to $200K — above that needs CFO (Linda Park) approval. At $380K this requires CFO sign-off and James wants to avoid that conversation unless he’s confident in the value story.
follow_ups: Has James met with Linda Park about this renewal yet?
What does Linda Park typically need to see to approve SaaS spend at this level?

questions.text: What would make you feel confident recommending renewal to your CFO?
questions.answer: Three things: 1) A clear ROI story with Evergreen-specific data, 2) A concrete plan to improve adoption at the 6 lagging hospitals, 3) Ideally a reference call with a similar health system that renewed. He was very specific about these.
follow_ups: Is there a format or template Linda Park prefers for vendor ROI presentations?
Do you have a timeline for when you’d want to go to Linda — before or after we meet with Maria Santos?

questions.text: Are you evaluating any competing solutions, or is this primarily a renewal decision?
questions.answer: Not actively evaluating alternatives. But James said if they can’t justify the spend, they’d consider building something lightweight in-house using Teams and Power BI. Low probability but real — their IT team has done internal builds before.
follow_ups: Has the internal build option been scoped at all, or is it theoretical?
What would the internal build lack compared to what they have today?

questions.text: What’s the biggest risk you see to renewing?
questions.answer: Getting CFO buy-in without a clear ROI story. James said Linda Park is skeptical of SaaS spending he can’t quantify. He specifically needs readmission rate impact data tied to platform usage before he’d feel comfortable going to her.
follow_ups: Is there a deadline for when you’d need the ROI data to make the June 1 timeline work?
Would Linda Park want to meet directly with our team, or does James prefer to present solo?

questions.text: Dr. Kim was your champion — who’s filled that role internally since she left?
questions.answer: No one officially. James has inherited the vendor relationship but he wasn’t the original buyer. He’s trying to get up to speed but doesn’t have clinical context. There’s a gap between IT and clinical leadership right now.
follow_ups: Does James have a relationship with the new CMO (Dr. Walsh)?
Is there anyone on the clinical side who has been advocating informally?

questions.text: Is there a clinical champion we should try to identify and engage — a nursing director or medical director?
questions.answer: James suggested Director of Care Coordination, Maria Santos. She’s at Evergreen Main (one of the high-adoption sites) and has been informally advocating for the tool. James offered to make an intro if we want to connect with her.
follow_ups: What’s Maria’s level of influence with clinical leadership?
Would she be willing to present at an internal leadership meeting on the platform’s impact?

questions.text: How has the leadership change affected IT’s priorities — is care coordination still a strategic priority?
questions.answer: It’s dropped a tier. The new CMO (Dr. Robert Walsh) is focused on the Epic optimization roadmap and hasn’t engaged with our product at all. IT is stretched thin and there’s no clinical executive pushing this forward.
follow_ups: Is Dr. Walsh’s Epic focus a short-term initiative or a multi-year program?
Would positioning our platform as complementary to Epic (not competitive) land better with him?

questions.text: Who else should we be building a relationship with to make sure this renewal goes smoothly?
questions.answer: James suggested reconnecting with Maria Santos and scheduling a brief with Dr. Walsh to reintroduce the product. He’d support setting up both meetings. Also mentioned that Linda Park (CFO) will want to be briefed at some point before the June 1 date.
follow_ups: What’s the best way to request a meeting with Dr. Walsh — through James, or directly?
Is there a preferred timing for the CFO conversation relative to the renewal date?

meetings.title: Renewal Kickoff — Introduction to James Tran
meetings.notes:
First call since Dr. Kim's departure. James was briefed on the product but wasn't part of the original purchase and has no emotional investment in the original goals. Tone was neutral-to-cautious.

He flagged the 40% usage drop immediately and already understood the cause — Dr. Kim ran weekly training sessions and no one stepped in. He framed it as an internal failure, not a product problem. That's a good sign.

His ask was direct: he needs a ROI report with Evergreen-specific data before he'll take the renewal to his CFO (Linda Park). At $380K he's above his $200K authority threshold, so this conversation with Linda is unavoidable. He wants to go into it prepared.

Renewal date is June 1. Roughly 16 weeks to build a value story and close.

meetings.title: QBR — IT Leadership
meetings.attendees: James Tran, Rachel Kim (IT Manager), CSM, You (TAM)
meetings.notes:
Broader IT team review. Rachel manages day-to-day vendor relationships and came in more skeptical than James — she asked pointed questions about what adoption support we'd provide for the 6 lagging hospitals and whether SLA commitments were being met.

Big moment: I showed the analytics dashboard, which James had never seen before. Rachel immediately asked if they could pull a custom report on readmission rates tied to platform usage. That's exactly the CFO ROI hook we need to build.

Action items coming out of this call:
- Pull readmission rate data analysis from our platform and prepare a one-pager before end of March
- Get IT the updated iPad app version for the lagging hospitals (blocked on their IT push schedule)
- Identify if there's a clinical champion we can engage — James hinted at someone in care coordination at Evergreen Main

meetings.title: Discovery Deep-Dive — James + Maria Santos
meetings.attendees: James Tran (IT Director), Maria Santos (Director of Care Coordination, Evergreen Main), You (TAM)
meetings.notes:
Best call of the renewal cycle. Maria is exactly the internal champion we needed. She's been using the platform daily at Evergreen Main, has strong opinions, and is clearly respected by James.

Maria confirmed the two-hospital adoption split and gave specific color: the difference was nurse champions during rollout at Main and Eastside, nothing equivalent at the other 6. She volunteered to lead a training program for the lagging hospitals if we provide materials and a structured rollout plan.

James's demeanor shifted noticeably with Maria in the room. He agreed to facilitate introductions to Dr. Walsh (new CMO) and Linda Park (CFO) once we have the readmission ROI one-pager ready. He specifically said: “If you can show Linda the numbers, I'll set up the meeting.”

This is the call where the renewal moved from at-risk to recoverable. Maria Santos is now the internal champion. Next step: three-way working session to build the 90-day adoption plan for the 6 lagging hospitals.

key_findings: Original champion Dr. Sarah Kim departed in January, leaving no internal owner — renewal risk is primarily a relationship and change management problem, not product dissatisfaction
Usage dropped 40% since Q3, concentrated at 6 of 8 hospitals; Evergreen Main and Eastside are strong adopters with nurse champions driving daily engagement
CFO sign-off is required for renewal ($380K ARR exceeds IT Director’s $200K authority) — business case must be quantified with Evergreen-specific readmission rate data
IT Director James Tran is evaluating the product fresh without emotional investment in the original purchase decision — the original success metrics feel like someone else’s goals
Director of Care Coordination Maria Santos (Evergreen Main) is an informal champion and the strongest path to rebuilding internal clinical advocacy
technical_requirements: Epic bi-directional sync needs to be enabled at remaining 7 hospitals (currently active at only 1 of 8)
Mobile app update must be pushed to older hospital iPads — IT owns this and it’s blocking care coordinator adoption at multiple sites
Analytics dashboard walkthrough needed for James and Maria — readmission rate reporting is the specific output required for the CFO ROI story
Teams integration or surfacing mechanism should be explored to reduce context-switching friction cited as the primary reason for non-use
Potential integration planning for Q4 2026 clinic network acquisition if it proceeds
risks_and_concerns: No executive clinical champion — new CMO Dr. Walsh is focused on Epic roadmap and has not engaged with the platform; without clinical executive sponsorship, renewal is vulnerable to deprioritization
CFO Linda Park is skeptical of unquantified SaaS spend — without specific readmission rate impact data, the $380K renewal will stall at her desk
Build-internally alternative (Teams + Power BI) is the stated fallback if ROI cannot be demonstrated — low probability but credible given IT’s build history
No formal clinical onboarding process means adoption decay will continue as staff turnover occurs — structural problem that needs a joint solution
IT stretched thin with no bandwidth to drive platform rollout at 6 lagging hospitals without CSM-led structured support
recommended_next_steps: Pull readmission rate impact analysis this week and deliver a ready-to-present one-pager to James by end of month — this is the unlock for the CFO conversation
Book a three-way intro call: CSM + James + Maria Santos to establish Maria as the new clinical champion and co-create a 90-day adoption plan
Request a 30-minute executive briefing with Dr. Walsh (new CMO) through James — position as a quarterly partnership review, not a sales call; frame around Epic complementarity
Identify a peer reference customer in a similar health system who renewed and improved adoption — arrange a reference call for James before he goes to Linda Park
Build and deliver a 90-day adoption recovery plan: Epic sync enablement at 7 remaining hospitals, iPad update coordination with IT, and nurse champion playbook for the 6 lagging sites
summary.raw_text:
Evergreen Health Systems is approaching renewal at $380K ARR in a materially different position than when they originally purchased. The January departure of Dr. Sarah Kim — the original executive champion — has created an ownership vacuum that is the root cause of every risk signal we’re seeing: usage decline, lack of internal advocacy, and a CFO who has never heard a compelling story about this investment.

James Tran is a reasonable counterpart who wants to renew if we can make it easy for him to do so. He has been direct about exactly what he needs: a quantified ROI story tied to readmission rates, a credible adoption recovery plan for the 6 lagging hospitals, and a peer reference call. These are achievable in the next 30–45 days if we move with urgency.

The product itself is not the problem. The two hospitals with engaged nurse champions — Evergreen Main and Eastside — are strong daily users. This validates the product and gives us a compelling internal case study. Maria Santos at Evergreen Main is an untapped asset who has been informally advocating for the platform; she should become our primary clinical champion immediately and be central to the adoption recovery plan.

The path to a successful renewal has three lanes running in parallel: (1) deliver the CFO-ready readmission impact data story within two weeks, (2) establish Maria Santos as the new internal champion with a joint 90-day adoption plan, and (3) secure a brief introductory meeting with the new CMO Dr. Walsh before the June 1 deadline. Executing on all three converts a churn risk into a stable renewal and positions us well for the potential clinic network expansion in Q4 2026.
